content: Suppose the mean and the standard deviation of the data $\left\{x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_9\right\}$ are $\mu$ and $\sigma(\neq 0)$, respectively. After including one more data value $x_{10}$, the mean of the data $\left\{x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_9, x_{10}\right\}$ remains $\mu$. What is the standard deviation of the data $\left\{x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_9, x_{10}\right\}$ ?
explanation:
To simplify and clarify the explanation on updating the standard deviation when a new data point is added, consider the following:

Mean Calculation:

Original Data Mean:

The mean of the original data set $\left\{x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_9\right\}$ is given by:

$ \mu = \frac{1}{9} \sum_{i=1}^9 x_i \implies \sum_{i=1}^9 x_i = 9\mu $

New Data Set Mean:

When a new data point $x_{10}$ is added, maintaining the same mean $\mu$:

$ \mu = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = \frac{1}{10}\left(9\mu + x_{10}\right) $

Solving for $x_{10}$:

$ 10\mu = 9\mu + x_{10} \implies x_{10} = \mu $

Standard Deviation Calculation:

Original Standard Deviation:

The standard deviation of the original data set is:

$ \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{9} \sum_{i=1}^9 (x_i - \mu)^2} $

Calculate Variance of the New Data Set:

The variance for the new data set is:

$ \text{Var} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{i=1}^{10} (x_i - \mu)^2 $

Breaking it down:

$ \sum_{i=1}^{10} (x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^9 (x_i - \mu)^2 + (x_{10} - \mu)^2 $

Since $x_{10} = \mu$, $(x_{10} - \mu)^2 = 0$. Thus:

$ \sum_{i=1}^{10} (x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^9 (x_i - \mu)^2 $

Equating Variance Values:

Since $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{9} \sum_{i=1}^9 (x_i - \mu)^2$, we have:

$ \sum_{i=1}^9 (x_i - \mu)^2 = 9\sigma^2 $

Substitute in new variance formula:

$ \text{Var} = \frac{1}{10} \cdot 9\sigma^2 = \frac{9}{10}\sigma^2 $

New Standard Deviation:

The new standard deviation is:

$ \sqrt{\text{Var}} = \sqrt{\frac{9}{10}\sigma^2} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{10}}\sigma = \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{10}} $

Thus, the standard deviation of the updated data set $\left\{x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_9, x_{10}=\mu\right\}$ is $\frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{10}}$.
